does: This paper investigates why sparse autoencoders (SAEs) show varying reconstruction error across different layers of neural networks, challenging the assumption of a single universal scaling law. The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study fitting 844 SAE checkpoints across 68 layers of Gemma 2 models, developing a two-stage analysis: first fitting per-layer scaling-law surfaces relating SAE width and sparsity to reconstruction error, then regressing the fitted parameters against geometric properties of activation manifolds. They find that layerwise variation in reconstruction error correlates with manifold curvature and intrinsic dimensionality, demonstrating that SAE scaling is fundamentally a function of local geometric structure rather than a global constant across layers.
connects: This work extends research on sparse autoencoders as tools for mechanistic interpretability, building on the linear representation hypothesis that activation spaces can be decomposed into sparse linear combinations of interpretable features. It responds to limitations in existing SAE scaling laws, which typically fit single-layer models without explaining cross-layer variation. The paper connects to broader literature on neural network geometry, manifold learning, and the structure of learned representations. It also engages with recent work on Gemma Scope SAEs and scaling law empiricism, combining insights from interpretability research with differential geometry to explain why locally-fitted power laws alone cannot capture SAE behavior.
enables: This geometric perspective on SAE scaling opens several directions: it suggests that SAE design and training should account for layerwise manifold properties rather than treating all layers uniformly, potentially improving reconstruction and interpretability. The framework provides a methodology for predicting where SAEs will succeed or fail based on activation geometry rather than post-hoc fitting. It could inform architecture design or SAE initialization strategies that adapt to manifold structure. Beyond SAEs, the finding that manifold geometry predicts scaling behavior may generalize to other compression or representation-learning tasks in deep networks, and the cross-layer geometric analysis provides tools for characterizing how neural networks organize information hierarchically.
